edges.description: BCL2 overexpression prevents deletion of germinal center B cells
Oncogenic BCL2 expression helps establish long-lived precursor clones
Anti-apoptotic signaling allows precursor and tumor B cells to persist long enough to accumulate additional lesions
Distorted chromatin programs stabilize malignant identity and support ongoing clonal diversification
edges.source: Epigenetic Deregulation of Germinal Center Program

edges.description: Sustained trophic signaling from the niche promotes relapse-prone survival and immune escape
Persistent BCR signaling supports ongoing adaptation and outgrowth of FL clones

nodes.description: Constitutive BCL2 expression blocks the mitochondrial apoptosis program, allowing germinal center B cells that would normally be deleted during the germinal center reaction to survive as lymphoma precursors.

evidence.reference_title: Recent advances in understanding the biology of follicular lymphoma.
evidence.snippet: BCL2 overexpression by t(14;18) confers a survival advantage to B cells during the germinal center reaction

nodes.description: In most cases, the hallmark t(14;18)(q32;q21)/IGH::BCL2 rearrangement places BCL2 under immunoglobulin enhancer control, driving constitutive BCL2 overexpression in germinal center B cells.

evidence.reference_title: Bridging clinicopathologic features and genetics in follicular lymphoma: Towards enhanced diagnostic accuracy and subtype differentiation.
evidence.snippet: Approximately 85% of FL cases harbor the t(14;18)(q32;q21)/IGH::BCL2 which leads to the overexpression of BCL2.

nodes.description: FL immunoglobulins often acquire N-linked glycosylation motifs that support chronic B-cell receptor signaling. This persistent signaling promotes dark-zone polarization and ongoing clonal evolution.

evidence.reference_title: Recent advances in understanding the biology of follicular lymphoma.
evidence.snippet: FL cells frequently carry N-linked glycosylation motifs within the immunoglobulin gene, leading to chronic activation of the B-cell receptor (BCR). Recent evidence suggests that this chronic BCR signaling drives FL polarization toward a dark-zone phenotype and promotes clonal evolution.

nodes.description: The combination of anti-apoptotic signaling, epigenetic deregulation, and microenvironmental support, and chronic BCR signaling allows FL clones to survive for years, relapse after therapy, and occasionally transform into aggressive large B-cell lymphoma.

FL commonly carries recurrent mutations in chromatin-modifying genes including KMT2D, CREBBP, and EZH2. These lesions distort transcriptional programs required for normal germinal center exit and differentiation, reinforcing the malignant state.
nodes.id: Epigenetic Deregulation of Germinal Center Program

evidence.reference_title: Bridging clinicopathologic features and genetics in follicular lymphoma: Towards enhanced diagnostic accuracy and subtype differentiation.
evidence.snippet: These neoplasms often exhibit hallmark epigenetic deregulation due to recurrent mutations in genes such as KMT2D, CREBBP, and EZH2

evidence.reference_title: Recent advances in understanding the biology of follicular lymphoma.
evidence.snippet: abnormalities in epigenetic modifier genes lead to desynchronization of gene expression changes in germinal center B cells.

nodes.description: FL cells remain highly dependent on the lymph node microenvironment. Reciprocal signaling with follicular helper T cells, stromal cells, and other immune cells provides survival, adhesion, and immune-evasive signals that preserve tumor fitness.

evidence.reference_title: Integrative Analysis of Cell Crosstalk within Follicular Lymphoma Cell Niche: Towards a Definition of the FL Supportive Synapse.
evidence.snippet: Follicular lymphoma (FL), the most frequent indolent non-Hodgkin's B cell lymphoma, is considered as a prototypical centrocyte-derived lymphoma, dependent on a specific microenvironment mimicking the normal germinal center (GC).
